hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: The property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, the property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. First adapted to institutional use in 1918, the property is also associated with the development of the District as a complete community that included residential, institutional, civic, and commercial uses. The property retains many of its original building features, including its cross-gabled roof, metal fascia and boxed cornice in its dormer gable, fenestration pattern with flat-headed openings, continuous lintels and concrete sills, first-storey bay window, decorative wooden bargeboards, and brick cladding with decorative brickwork and terracotta panel insets. Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. The property is important in maintaining and supporting the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

facade_detail.composition: The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 3 storeys and is capped by a gable, consistent with its house-form, detached typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2, 1 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_expression: Character-defining facade elements include decorative brick, original windows, cornice.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: The property retains many of its original building features, including its cross-gabled roof, metal fascia and boxed cornice in its dormer gable, fenestration pattern with flat-headed openings, continuous lintels and concrete sills, first-storey bay window, decorative wooden bargeboards, and brick cladding with decorative brickwork and terracotta panel insets. Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.

facade_description: Detached three-storey house-form in brick with a gable roof. Features include a double-height three-sided projecting bay, decorative bargeboards and a decorative cornice. Institutional conversion from residential. Pre-1889 construction.
heritage_summary: The property retains many of its original building features, including its cross-gabled roof, metal fascia and boxed cornice in its dormer gable, fenestration pattern with flat-headed openings, continuous lintels and concrete sills, first-storey bay window, decorative wooden bargeboards, and brick cladding with decorative brickwork and terracotta panel insets. Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.
